title: GE Vernova Hitachi’s SMR design speeds through UK regulatory process | New Civil Engineer
text:
UK regulators have cleared the GE Vernova Hitachi BWRX‑300 small modular reactor (SMR) design to move forward in the Generic Design Assessment process in record time.
This means it has been concluded there are no fundamental safety, security, safeguards or environmental protection shortfalls that would bar its deployment in England and Wales.
The Office for Nuclear Regulation (ONR), the Environment Agency and Natural Resources Wales said the design had completed Step 2 of the two‑step GDA, which follows an initial year‑long initiation phase that began in January 2024. Step 2, started in December 2024, involved a detailed review of the reactor’s design and supporting documentation.
Regulators issued two GDA statements setting out their conclusions and confirmed that, on the basis of the material assessed, the BWRX‑300’s fundamental design features are adequate from a regulatory perspective. They stressed, however, that this outcome does not constitute approval to build: further, more detailed assessments would be required before any safety‑significant construction could begin or environmental permits be granted.
The BWRX‑300 is a boiling‑water SMR developed by GE Vernova with Hitachi. The companies promote modular construction techniques and compact plant layouts; GE Vernova has said the design could be built in roughly 24–36 months.
At present there are no plans to deploy the BWRX‑300 in England and Wales and no potential sites have been identified. If a developer wished to proceed, regulators said they would either return to the GDA for a Step 3 assessment with GE Vernova Hitachi or carry out a detailed, site‑specific assessment with a prospective licence holder or constructor.
The GDA is a voluntary, front‑loaded process that allows UK nuclear regulators to examine new reactor designs in advance of any site applications. By separating design assessment from planning and site consent, the process aims to give potential developers and the public early confidence that a reactor concept can meet UK safety, security and environmental standards.
While there are no concrete plans currently to deploy this SMR design in England and Wales, GE Vernova Hitachi is set to deploy its technology in Poland and Eastern Europe through private entity Orlen Synthos Green Energy (OGSE). As such, OGSE coinvested in the GDA for the BWRX-300 as part of the UK government’s Future Nuclear Enabling Fund project to benefit from regulatory lessons learned.
Additionally, construction of the first BWRX-300 is underway at Ontario Power Generation’s (OPG) Darlington site near Toronto. A total of four units are planned for the site.
Regulators hail ‘quickest GDA engagement completed to date’
Saffron Price-Finnerty, the Environment Agency’s new reactors programme manager, said: “The accelerated pace of this first two-Step GDA was enabled by the delivery of a complete set of documentation by GE Vernova Hitachi at the start of Step 2. This helped our assessment team to plan their work effectively and efficiently.
“Their assessments were targeted and proportionate in ensuring that there were no fundamental shortfalls in environmental protection. It has been a considerable effort from both the Requesting Party and regulators, resulting in a quality outcome in such a short timeframe.”
“As the environmental regulator of nuclear sites and radioactive substances in England, the Environment Agency ensures that nuclear companies and the sites they operate meet high standards of environmental protection throughout the stages of design, construction, operation and decommissioning.”
Rob Exley, ONR’s head of the BWRX-300 GDA, said: “GE Vernova Hitachi opted for a shorter two-step GDA, making it the first requesting party to take advantage of the flexibility we introduced in our modernised GDA process.
“This means it is the quickest GDA engagement completed to date, facilitated by GE Vernova Hitachi’s responsiveness, the quality and maturity of its submissions, learning from previous GDAs, and our active collaboration with regulatory colleagues in the US and Canada who have been evaluating the BWRX-300 in parallel to our assessment.”
Paul Gibson, Natural Resources Wales nuclear team leader, said: “Throughout Step 2 we have worked closely with the Environment Agency and Office for Nuclear Regulation towards the fundamental assessment of the GE Vernova Hitachi SMR, resulting in the successful delivery of this first two-step GDA.”
GE Vernova Hitachi’s UK country leader Andy Champ also commented on the speed of this “significant regulatory milestone”, saying: “This is the fastest that any technology provider has completed steps 1 and 2, truly demonstrating the value of our progress in construction in Canada and is another sign that the BWRX-300 continues to advance globally.”
